Title: Genome assembly and chemogenomic profiling of National Flower of Singapore Papilionanthe Miss Joaquim ‘Agnes’ reveals metabolic pathways regulating floral traits

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Glucosyltransferase candidates are proposed to complete vandateroside biosynthesis by glucosylating the p-hydroxybenzyl moiety.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Glucosyltransferase candidates from the flavonoid pathway are abundant across perianth tissues.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific UGT genes were not resolved; role is putative and inferred from pathway logic plus metabolite detection.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Vandateroside structure suggests a required glucosylation step. 2. PMJ contains glucosyltransferase candidates abundant in perianth tissues. 3. These candidates were proposed to participate in vandateroside biosynthesis, but no single gene was functionally validated.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome annotation; RNA-seq; metabolomics (LC-HRMS); BioProject PRJNA764569
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Pathway completion requires identification of the exact UGT(s) before engineering use.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome mining; transcript profiling; LC-HRMS metabolite detection

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Elevated MADS6 expression is associated with mature labellum regulation rather than a directly demonstrated nutrient metabolite output.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Enhanced expression in flower labellum during mature bloom.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Enhanced expression in flower labellum during mature bloom.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a floral trait regulator highlighted in Results, but not directly validated as a nutrient metabolism regulator; metabolite causality is weak.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MADS6 was highly expressed in the labellum during mature bloom. 2. The study inferred a role in mature labellum regulation. 3. Direct regulation of specific nutrient-metabolism genes was not shown.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential floral organ trait regulator but not yet a direct metabolic engineering target from this study.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: HMM-based gene identification; phylogenetic classification; RNA-seq expression profiling

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Elevated specialized MADS16 expression is associated with mature labellum regulation rather than directly demonstrated metabolite production.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Enhanced expression in flower labellum during mature bloom.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Enhanced expression in flower labellum during mature bloom.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because it was emphasized in Results, but no direct nutrient-metabolism target genes or metabolites were established.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MADS16 was highly expressed in the labellum during mature bloom. 2. The study suggested its involvement in specialized mature labellum regulation. 3. Direct causal linkage to a defined nutrient metabolite was not shown.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential floral organ development regulator, not a proven metabolism target in this paper.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: HMM-based gene identification; phylogenetic classification; RNA-seq expression profiling

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Core flavonoid pathway upstream of anthocyanidin formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates flavanones to form dihydroflavonols in the flavonoid pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher F3H expression in floral tissues is associated with anthocyanin biosynthesis supporting flower pigmentation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher expression in perianth, labellum and gynostemium compared with other tissues (Log2FC 4.7, padj 3.12E-17).
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher expression in perianth, labellum and gynostemium compared with other tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq differential expression; metabolite profiling by LC-HRMS
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Floral pigmentation; antioxidant activity; developmental regulation; insect attraction; UV and pathogen protection
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for engineering floral color intensity together with downstream anthocyanin genes.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified among 62 anthocyanin-biosynthesis-related enzymes in the PMJ genome.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. F3H showed significantly elevated expression in perianth, labellum and gynostemium tissues. 2. Its expression pattern supports active flavonoid flux toward anthocyanin formation in pigmented floral organs. 3. It was inferred from transcriptome-metabolome integration rather than direct enzyme assay.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts leucoanthocyanidins into anthocyanidins, precursors of anthocyanin pigments.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher ANS expression coincides with anthocyanin pigment accumulation and reddish-purple floral coloration.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Labellum; dorsal sepal; lateral petal; gynostemium
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher expression in perianth and labellum compared with other tissues (Log2FC 6, padj 1.46E-25).
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher expression in perianth and labellum compared with other tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point for anthocyanin formation inferred in this study
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq differential expression; LC-HRMS metabolite profiling of anthocyanin derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Floral pigmentation; antioxidant activity; developmental regulation; insect attraction; UV and pathogen protection
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate target for modifying orchid floral pigmentation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper explicitly states presence of anthocyanin pigments coincides with ANS expression.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANS was highly expressed in pigmented floral tissues, especially perianth and labellum. 2. Anthocyanin derivatives detected by LC-HRMS matched the floral pigmentation phenotype. 3. ANS expression was used as a central molecular explanation for anthocyanin-based color formation in PMJ.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Putative vandateroside / eucomic acid derivative biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Eucomic acid ester precursor for vandaterosides (putative)
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Proposed to catalyze esterification involving eucomic acid and p-hydroxybenzyl alcohol during vandateroside precursor formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: A BAHD gene in PMJ is proposed to support formation of eucomic acid-derived precursor used for vandateroside biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf; floral tissues; gynostemium for eucomic acid localization
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: BAHD transcript detected across tissues; no significant differences across means were observed.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed across tissues without significant tissue-specific differences.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genome mining; transcript profiling; LC-HRMS metabolite detection
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome assembly; RNA-seq; metabolomics (LC-HRMS); BioProject PRJNA764569
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Potential anti-skin-aging activity via eucomic acid/vandateroside bioactivity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for metabolic engineering of vandateroside accumulation, but pathway remains putative and requires biochemical validation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Only a candidate gene was reported; catalytic role is inferred, not experimentally confirmed.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A BAHD acyltransferase gene was identified in the PMJ genome as a putative enzyme for eucomic acid-related esterification. 2. Eucomic acid and vandaterosides I-III were detected by LC-HRMS across PMJ tissues. 3. The proposed role remains hypothetical without direct enzyme assay.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Stilbene core compounds including pinosylvin or resveratrol-related intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses cinnamoyl-CoA or p-coumaroyl-CoA with three malonyl-CoA units to produce stilbene skeletons.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Presence of stilbene synthase genes supports production of antimicrobial stilbenoids detected in PMJ tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Stress and physical damage are noted to mediate pinosylvin production
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Antimicrobial defense; potential antibiotic-enhancing activity for resveratrol
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate pathway gene for enhancing bioactive stilbenoid content in orchids.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Genes were identified in the genome; direct expression-functional coupling was not deeply validated in Results.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Stilbene synthase genes were identified in the PMJ genome. 2. Corresponding stilbenoids pinosylvin and resveratrol were detected by LC-HRMS. 3. The study inferred these genes underlie defensive stilbenoid biosynthesis in PMJ.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Hydroperoxy fatty acid intermediates leading to C9 aldehydes and jasmonate-related volatiles
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygenates polyunsaturated fatty acids to initiate formation of green leaf volatiles and related oxylipins.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Presence of LOX genes supports production of oxygenated hydrocarbon volatiles contributing to PMJ scent profile.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Tissue damage can activate green leaf volatile production and release
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to alter volatile profile, though only inferred from gene presence in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Study identified putative LOX genes but did not provide detailed expression analysis for specific LOX loci.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Putative LOX genes were identified in the PMJ genome. 2. GC-HRMS detected oxygenated hydrocarbon volatiles consistent with LOX-derived products. 3. Results suggest PMJ is not truly scentless but produces low-level green leaf volatiles.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Asymmetrically cleaves the polyene backbone of β-carotene at the C9-C10 position to generate β-ionone.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhanced CCD7 expression in labellum and gynostemium indicates local β-ionone biosynthesis contributing to PMJ floral scent.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Significantly enriched in labellum and gynostemium (Log2FC 6.6, padj 2.19E-20).
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Significantly enriched in labellum and gynostemium.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: terminal determinant for β-ionone formation in this context
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq differential expression; GC-HRMS volatile profiling
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible diversion of carotenoid pool toward volatile apocarotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Floral scent; ecological signaling; attraction of pollinators; insect repulsion
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for engineering floral aroma and pollinator-attraction traits via apocarotenoid volatiles.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper highlights CCD7 as the enzyme responsible for β-ionone production in PMJ floral tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Low levels of β-ionone were detected in PMJ flowers. 2. CCD7 expression was strongly enriched in labellum and gynostemium. 3. Transcript-metabolite concordance supports CCD7-mediated β-ionone biosynthesis as part of PMJ scent.
